paragraphs.body: All In

Some experiment, a weird cult, a prank. These were Sloan’s best guesses at who was running this gameI. The walls were a dull, sterile white with a complete lack of decoration or texture. She sat in the center of the room at a table with six other people. Five of them were dressed like her – in casual street clothes. The dealer at the front of the table, however, was dressed in a crisp, refined suit, his face obscured with a cheap mask. She wondered if the man arrived here like her, but idle speculation wouldn’t help her right now, no. She needed her eyes on the players, her mind on their cards. She needed to focus.

annotations.comment: I like how you put us into the world of the story right away.
Like this swoop into her thinking. I’m intrigued by the game (although I confess I didn’t really buy the set up — feels like a movie, not real life).

paragraphs.body: Sloan looked down at her own cards: Two aces and a three. Another ace in the community hand meant she had a good chance of winning this round. Her hand drifted to her chips, strange obsidian cubes all perfectly cut to the same dimensions. The chips were small, barely more than the size of a marble, and the edges were sharp and precise.
“Call,” she said, gathering three chips out of her stack and pushing it to the center.

The cubes slowly slid away from her and into the pot, a gentle tugging sensation in her chest accompanying the movement. The farther the chips grew from her, the more demanding the sensation became. She felt the restraints tying her to the chair keeping her planted, and so she had to stretch to reach the entire way. Her chips now in the center, she braced herself for the sharp twang of emptiness that struck every time she offered more.

annotations.comment: I can’t tell if this is just her response to betting or if these chips are actually evoking this response from her.

paragraphs.body: The dealer flipped the final card and the players’ hands were revealed. Just as she suspected, she won this round, feeling a warm sensation in her stomach as the chips returned to her. She now sat at 48, if she had been counting correctly. The dealer collected all of their cards, shuffled, and dealt a new hand. Then began another round of betting.

annotations.comment: You nicely set up this world but I think you can get these details to work harder for you. I’m still not sure if what I read was metaphor or an actual description of what was happening to Sloan.

paragraphs.body: An older man with a stack of chips that rivaled all of the other players–a stack that began larger than anyone else’s–started the round. He had graying hair in a short, professional cut and wrinkles that just so happened to emphasize his best features. He looked at everyone else’s stacks before moving. He grabbed one chip, then another, then another.
“Ten,” he said after gathering them all.

Silence. No one had bet more than six at a time in the countless rounds that they had already played. Hesitantly, the blonde woman next to him–Helena, Sloan had learned–took her turn, calling the bet. Everyone else followed suit until it was the turn of the man next to her, Marcus. Marcus was a stout man with tussled black hair and a five o’clock shadow. He looked around at everyone else, his eyes pleading for help. Sloan looked at him and then looked down. He only had seven chips left. Everyone waited silently for him to say something.

annotations.comment: Can combine to avoid the repetition. Just noting that I’m about 30% of the way into the story, and while I am enjoying it, I’m not sure what the plot is. I assume Sloan wants to win some money but I don’t know yet if she HAS to or just WANTS to do so.

paragraphs.body: “Your bet, sir,” the masked dealer prompted.
“I don’t have enough,” he replied shakily.
“Then I’m afraid you must go all-in.”

Marcus thought for a long time. His eyes darted around the room as he contemplated his options. His hand moved to rest on his last chip. He picked it up in his hands, clenching it tight and turning his palm to the ceiling. He reached with his trembling hand to the center of the table. He slowly turned his hand over releasing the chip, and as the chip fell, so too did Marcus, collapsing onto the table, silent and still.

After recovering from her shock, Sloan looked to the old man in anger for placing such a reckless bet and caught the corner of his mouth twitching in amusement. His eyes weren’t even on the man anymore, he was watching the terrified woman next to him, Valeria.

annotations.comment: I’m a little confused about where Valeria is. Could this be Helen instead?

paragraphs.body: “What the hell is wrong with him?” Valeria yelled.
“Marcus has run out, and is no longer permitted to play,” replied the dealer in a calm, detached voice.
“Is… Is he okay?” Helena asked quietly. Sloan reached out for the man’s wrist, but the chairs were spaced too far apart for her to reach.
“He’s breathing,” she offered.
“Help him!” Valeria shouted at the dealer.
“Now now, everyone. Let’s focus on the game once more. Any further delays will be met with a reduction of chips.”

annotations.text: Now now, everyone. Let’s focus on the game once more. Any further delays will be met with a reduction of chips.”
annotations.comment: I don’t quite follow this — I think you need some further reason why everyone would continue to play than just the dealer being snotty.

paragraphs.body: The dealer raised his hand, beckoning to someone Sloan couldn’t see. Eventually, someone moved behind Marcus’ seat, lifting him back up into his chair. She looked over to Marcus and was surprised to see his eyes were still open, though they were now dull and lifeless. The rounds continued, noticeably more tense than before. Slowly the chips dwindled as the old man made expensive bets the other players had no choice but to call. There was no folding in this game.

annotations.text: There was no folding in this game.
annotations.comment: Is this a turn from earlier hands? Presumably at some point some one went out? Or no?

paragraphs.body: Helena was the next to run out of chips, collapsing onto the table just as Marcus did. Valeria was next, and only the old man and Sloan remained. They sat across from one another, an audience of lifeless spectators watching the final rounds. They made occasional eye contact, but neither acknowledged the other. Sloan was sweating now, aware of her impossible position. What could she even do? She made a bet, he took it. The bigger the bet, the smarmier the face. And if she did win a round, so what? It’s not like he would be going anywhere anytime soon with his massive stockpile of chips.

annotations.comment: Is Sloan aware that this was how this game was going to go, that she could lose her life from playing it? I can’t tell yet if the rewards are so good that it’s worth taking the risk, or if she thought she was playing in a normal game but it’s turned bad.

paragraphs.body: Regardless, she soldiered on. She made calculated bets; she took calculated risks, but despite her efforts, she simply couldn’t handle the expensive bets the man kept making. Her chip count slowly dwindled and with it, her energy. She felt terrible, like she was being slowly carved out with each chip lost. Her heel tapped a staccato beat on the ground as her hands shook. She hadn’t been this low since she started, and she forgot just how bad it felt. Like you were missing something crucial. The chips continued to dwindle until she was down to her very last. She grabbed the chip. She felt the sharp points of the cube, tracing her finger along each edge. With a resigned sigh, she reached forward, dropped the chip, and fell.

annotations.comment: I enjoyed this story draft, Cameron. You evoked a creepy sense of destiny and I was interested to see what would happen (even if the ending, as it is now, is a bit of a letdown). I think you can help yourself by making the stakes (heh) for Sloan more clear. If she’s just playing for fun and ended up in this high stakes game sort of by mistake, well, that’s one thing. But if she needs money to pay off her student loans or look after her mom, that’s something else. That will help us care. I also would like to see Sloan fight a bit more. Right now, she keeps playing the game until she inevitably loses and then she dies (or whatever). That’s fine, she doesn’t have to win, but perhaps give her the chance to try to do so. What might she do to get out of this horrid situation? She wants, I assume, to live — how does she fight for that?A great first draft!
